title: First Address to the Syrian People as Transitional Leader

paragraphs.text: In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Today I stand before you, the great people of Syria, at a moment that history will remember for generations. After years of unimaginable suffering and sacrifice, we have arrived at a new dawn for our nation. This is not the triumph of one faction or one group — it is the triumph of the Syrian people's will to live in dignity and freedom.

Let me be clear from the outset: the era of tyranny is over, and the era of accountability has begun. But accountability does not mean vengeance. We must build a state of law, where justice is administered through institutions, not through the barrel of a gun. The courts, not the streets, will determine the fate of those who committed crimes against our people.

Syria belongs to all Syrians — to the Arab, the Kurd, the Turkmen, the Assyrian, the Druze, the Circassian. It belongs to the Muslim, the Christian, the Alawite, and every citizen who calls this ancient land home. No community will be marginalized, and no community will be granted supremacy over another. Our diversity is not a weakness to be exploited; it is the foundation upon which we will rebuild.

I know that many of you are watching with cautious hope and understandable skepticism. You have been promised change before, only to see those promises broken. I ask you to judge us not by our words today but by our actions in the months ahead. This transitional period will be defined by transparency, consultation, and measurable progress toward free elections.

Our first priority is restoring basic services to every Syrian household. Electricity, clean water, healthcare, and education must reach every city, every town, every village — from Daraa in the south to Qamishli in the northeast. We are working with international organizations to deliver emergency relief, but sustainable recovery requires rebuilding our own institutions from the ground up.

To the millions of Syrians living in exile — in Turkey, in Lebanon, in Jordan, in Europe, and across the world — I say: Syria awaits your return. We understand that return must be voluntary, safe, and dignified. We are establishing a commission to facilitate repatriation, to resolve property disputes fairly, and to ensure that no returning citizen faces persecution.

The question of transitional justice is perhaps the most delicate challenge we face. More than half a million of our brothers and sisters perished. Millions were displaced. Tens of thousands were detained and tortured in regime prisons. We owe the victims and their families truth, acknowledgment, and justice. A national truth and reconciliation commission will be established within sixty days.

At the same time, we extend a genuine hand of reconciliation to those who served in the former government's civilian institutions — teachers, engineers, doctors, municipal workers. The state cannot function without their expertise. Those who did not participate in crimes against the Syrian people will be welcomed back into public service after a transparent vetting process.

On the question of sovereignty, let me speak plainly. Syria's territorial integrity is non-negotiable. We will engage in dialogue with all domestic stakeholders regarding governance structures, but the unity of Syrian land is a red line. At the same time, we recognize that decentralization and local governance are necessary to reflect the will of communities across our diverse nation.

To our neighbors and to the international community, we seek partnerships based on mutual respect and non-interference. Syria will not be a staging ground for threats against any neighboring state, and we expect the same in return. We are ready for a new chapter of diplomatic engagement — one that serves the interests of the Syrian people above all else.

The presence of foreign forces on Syrian soil is a matter we will address through diplomacy and negotiation. We call on all parties with military forces in Syria to engage in a structured dialogue about withdrawal timelines. Syria's security must ultimately be guaranteed by Syrians, through a national army that represents all components of our society.

Our economic situation is dire, and I will not pretend otherwise. The Syrian pound has lost most of its value. Inflation has devastated household incomes. Our infrastructure lies in ruins across vast stretches of the country. Recovery will take years, perhaps decades. But we have seen nations rise from far worse devastation, and with the determination of our people and the support of the international community, we will rebuild.

We call upon the international community to lift sanctions that harm ordinary Syrians. We understand that sanctions were imposed in response to the crimes of the former regime. That regime is gone. Continuing to punish the Syrian people for the sins of their former oppressors is neither just nor productive. We are prepared to meet every reasonable condition to facilitate sanctions relief.

To the young people of Syria — you who grew up knowing nothing but war, displacement, and deprivation — I want to say that this nation's future belongs to you. We will invest in education, in vocational training, in entrepreneurship. You deserve the chance to build careers, start families, and live with hope in your own country rather than risking your lives on dangerous journeys abroad.

To the women of Syria, who held families together through the darkest years, who ran schools in basements and clinics in besieged neighborhoods — you will have a seat at every table where decisions about Syria's future are made. Gender equality is not a Western import; it is a demand of justice and a necessity for our nation's development.

I am announcing today the formation of a transitional governing council that includes representatives from all regions and communities of Syria. This council will oversee the drafting of a new constitution through an inclusive consultative process. The constitution will be put to the Syrian people in a national referendum, followed by free and fair elections under international observation.

We set a timeline of twenty-four months for the transitional period. Within this window, we aim to complete the constitutional process and hold elections. I pledge before you and before God that I will respect the outcome of those elections. Power belongs to the people, and it will be returned to the people through the ballot box.

Let us not forget the enormous sacrifices that brought us to this moment. Let us honor those sacrifices by building a Syria worthy of them — a Syria where no child goes to bed hungry, where no prisoner is tortured, where no family is torn apart by arbitrary power. This is our covenant with the martyrs and with future generations.

The road ahead is long, and it will be difficult. There will be setbacks and frustrations. But I have witnessed the resilience of the Syrian people through thirteen years of war, and I know that there is no force on earth stronger than a people united in pursuit of their freedom and dignity. Together, with God's help, we will rebuild Syria. Thank you, and may God bless Syria and its people.
